summary: Joel Spolsky announces the employer side of Stack Overflow Careers, a programmer search engine he's long wished for. He argues that the core hiring problem isn't evaluating programmers but finding them in the first place. The platform lets employers search for candidates by skill, location, and relocation willingness, with Stack Overflow reputation as a signal of programming ability. He acknowledges the chicken-and-egg challenge of building a two-sided marketplace and notes that early beta employers have already found promising candidates. The post serves as both a product launch announcement and a demonstration of the search functionality's practical value.
key_points: The hardest part of hiring programmers is finding candidates, not evaluating them in interviews
Stack Overflow Careers differentiates from traditional job boards by showing only active job seekers with verifiable programming skills via Stack Overflow reputation
Two-sided marketplaces face a chicken-and-egg problem requiring simultaneous critical mass of both employers and candidates
The platform reduces friction by letting employers search and see candidate counts before paying
Location flexibility and relocation willingness dramatically expand the candidate pool for any given search
money_quotes: The problem I've had with hiring programmers was not interviewing them or deciding if they're smart—it's been finding them in the first place.
What I've dreamed about is a programmer search engine.
If you've ever emailed someone based on a resume you found through a traditional search engine, you've probably discovered that they're not actually on the market.
It would show you something about their programming skills besides the usual resume blahblah.
It's like a junior high school dance, with the boys on one side of the gym and the girls on the other side, and for a while you just sit there holding your breath to see if anyone will dance.
key_insight: The biggest bottleneck in hiring programmers isn't the interview process but the discovery problem—finding qualified candidates who are actually looking for work—which a purpose-built search engine tied to a developer community can solve.
